category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 2 - Algebra, Complex Analysis, Topology/Part B - Complex Analysis

name.text: PYQ Part B - Cauchy-Riemann Equations

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y)\) be a complex function. The Cauchy-Riemann equations are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

The Cauchy-Riemann (CR) equations are \(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\) and \(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\). They are necessary conditions for complex differentiability. If the CR equations hold and the partial derivatives are continuous, then \(f\) is analytic (holomorphic).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Cauchy's Integral Formula

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(f\) is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\), and \(a\) is a point inside \(C\), then by Cauchy's Integral Formula:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Cauchy's Integral Formula: \(f(a) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C \dfrac{f(z)}{z-a}\,dz\). This shows that the value of an analytic function at any interior point is completely determined by its values on the boundary. The generalisation gives \(f^{(n)}(a) = \dfrac{n!}{2\pi i}\oint_C \dfrac{f(z)}{(z-a)^{n+1}}\,dz\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Liouville's Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Liouville's theorem in complex analysis states that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

Liouville's theorem: every bounded entire function is constant. An entire function is one analytic on all of \(\mathbb{C}\). The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra is a corollary: a non-constant polynomial has a root in \(\mathbb{C}\) (if not, \(1/p(z)\) would be bounded and entire, hence constant).

answer.text: Every analytic function on a domain is bounded
Every bounded entire function is constant

name.text: PYQ Part B - Residue Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(f\) is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\) except at finitely many isolated singularities \(z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n\) inside \(C\), then \(\oint_C f(z)\,dz\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Residue Theorem: \(\oint_C f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i \sum_{k=1}^n \text{Res}(f, z_k)\). The residue of \(f\) at a simple pole \(z_k\) is \(\lim_{z\to z_k}(z-z_k)f(z)\). This theorem is the main tool for evaluating real integrals via contour integration.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Essential Singularity (Casorati-Weierstrass)

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Near an essential singularity \(z_0\) of an analytic function \(f\), the image \(f(B(z_0, \delta) \setminus \{z_0\})\) for any \(\delta > 0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Casorati-Weierstrass theorem: near an essential singularity, the image is dense in \(\mathbb{C}\). The stronger Picard's Great Theorem says the image actually omits at most one complex value. Example: \(e^{1/z}\) has an essential singularity at \(0\) and takes every non-zero value infinitely many times near \(0\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Maximum Modulus Principle

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The Maximum Modulus Principle for analytic functions states that if \(f\) is non-constant and analytic on a bounded domain \(D\), then \(|f(z)|\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Maximum Modulus Principle: if \(f\) is analytic and non-constant on a bounded domain \(D\), then \(|f|\) attains its maximum on the boundary \(\partial D\), not in the interior. Equivalently, a non-constant analytic function has no interior maximum of its modulus. The minimum modulus principle (for \(f \neq 0\)) is analogous.

answer.text: Attains its maximum at an interior point of \(D\)
Attains its maximum on the boundary \(\partial D\)

name.text: PYQ Part B - Laurent Series Region

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The Laurent series of \(f(z)\) about a singularity \(z_0\) is valid in an annulus \(r < |z - z_0| < R\). If the principal part (terms with negative powers of \(z - z_0\)) has infinitely many non-zero terms, the singularity at \(z_0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

Classification via Laurent series: if the principal part has no terms, the singularity is removable; finitely many terms (up to \((z-z_0)^{-m}\)) means a pole of order \(m\); infinitely many terms means an essential singularity. Example: \(e^{1/z} = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{1}{n! z^n}\) has infinitely many negative-power terms at \(z=0\), so \(0\) is essential.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Mobius Transformation

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

A Möbius transformation (linear fractional transformation) \(w = \dfrac{az+b}{cz+d}\) with \(ad - bc \neq 0\) maps:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Möbius transformations map the extended complex plane \(\hat{\mathbb{C}} = \mathbb{C} \cup \{\infty\}\) to itself bijectively. They map circles and lines to circles and lines (lines are circles through \(\infty\)). They are conformal (angle-preserving) and form a group under composition.

answer.text: Circles and lines to circles and lines in \(\hat{\mathbb{C}}\)
The upper half-plane to the lower half-plane only

name.text: PYQ Part B - Analytic Function Constant if Real Part Constant

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(f = u + iv\) is analytic on a domain \(D\) and \(u\) is constant on \(D\), then \(f\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

If \(u = \text{const}\), then \(u_x = u_y = 0\). By CR equations: \(v_y = u_x = 0\) and \(v_x = -u_y = 0\). So \(v\) is also constant, and \(f = u + iv\) is constant. Similarly, if \(|f|\) is constant on a connected domain, then \(f\) is constant.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Argument Principle

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The Argument Principle states: if \(f\) is meromorphic inside a simple closed contour \(C\) with \(N\) zeros and \(P\) poles (counted with multiplicity) inside \(C\), then \(\dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C \dfrac{f'(z)}{f(z)}\,dz\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Argument Principle: \(\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C \frac{f'}{f}\,dz = N - P\), where \(N\) = number of zeros and \(P\) = number of poles inside \(C\). The left side equals the winding number of \(f(C)\) around 0, or \(\frac{1}{2\pi}\Delta_C \arg(f)\). Rouché's theorem is a corollary.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 2 - Algebra, Complex Analysis, Topology/Part B - Group Theory

name.text: PYQ Part B - Lagrange's Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Lagrange's theorem in group theory states that if \(H\) is a subgroup of a finite group \(G\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Lagrange's theorem: \(|H|\) divides \(|G|\), and \([G:H] = |G|/|H|\) is the index. The cosets of \(H\) partition \(G\) into \([G:H]\) disjoint equal-sized parts. Corollary: the order of any element divides \(|G|\). The converse is false in general (not every divisor of \(|G|\) gives a subgroup of that order).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Sylow First Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(G\) be a finite group and \(p\) a prime. If \(p^k\) divides \(|G|\), then \(G\) has a subgroup of order:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Sylow's First Theorem: if \(p^k \mid |G|\), then \(G\) has a subgroup of order \(p^k\). In particular, if \(p^m\) is the highest power of \(p\) dividing \(|G|\), there exists a subgroup of order \(p^m\) (a Sylow \(p\)-subgroup). The number of Sylow \(p\)-subgroups satisfies specific congruence conditions (Sylow's Third Theorem).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Normal Subgroup Characterization

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

A subgroup \(N\) of \(G\) is normal in \(G\) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

\(N \trianglelefteq G\) iff \(gNg^{-1} = N\) for all \(g \in G\), equivalently \(gN = Ng\) for all \(g\) (left cosets = right cosets). Normal subgroups are exactly the kernels of homomorphisms. The quotient group \(G/N\) is well-defined iff \(N\) is normal.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Order of Element in Z_n

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

In the cyclic group \(\mathbb{Z}_{12}\), the order of the element \(4\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

In \(\mathbb{Z}_n\), the order of element \(k\) is \(n/\gcd(n,k)\). Here: \(\gcd(12,4) = 4\), so order\((4) = 12/4 = 3\). Verify: \(4 + 4 + 4 = 12 \equiv 0 \pmod{12}\). Subgroups of \(\mathbb{Z}_{12}\) have orders \(1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12\) (divisors of 12).

name.text: PYQ Part B - First Isomorphism Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(\phi : G \to H\) be a group homomorphism. By the First Isomorphism Theorem:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

First Isomorphism Theorem: \(G/\ker\phi \cong \text{Im}(\phi)\). The quotient of \(G\) by the kernel is isomorphic to the image. Combined with Lagrange: \(|G| = |\ker\phi| \cdot |\text{Im}\,\phi|\) (for finite groups).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Centre of S_n for n ≥ 3

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The centre \(Z(S_n)\) of the symmetric group \(S_n\) for \(n \geq 3\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

For \(n \geq 3\), the centre \(Z(S_n) = \{e\}\) (trivial). Every non-identity permutation fails to commute with some other permutation. In contrast, \(Z(S_2) = S_2\). The alternating group \(A_n\) is simple for \(n \geq 5\), and \(Z(A_n) = \{e\}\) for \(n \geq 4\).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 2 - Algebra, Complex Analysis, Topology/Part B - Ring and Field Theory

name.text: PYQ Part B - Maximal Ideal in Z

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

In the ring \(\mathbb{Z}\) of integers, every maximal ideal is of the form:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

In \(\mathbb{Z}\), every ideal is of the form \((n) = n\mathbb{Z}\). An ideal \((p)\) is maximal iff \(\mathbb{Z}/(p)\) is a field, which happens iff \(p\) is prime. So maximal ideals in \(\mathbb{Z}\) are exactly the ideals generated by primes. \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z} \cong \mathbb{F}_p\) (the field with \(p\) elements).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Irreducibility by Eisenstein

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

By Eisenstein's criterion with the prime \(p = 2\), the polynomial \(x^4 + 2x^3 + 4x + 2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Eisenstein with \(p=2\): check that \(2 \nmid 1\) (leading coeff), \(2 \mid 2, 0, 4, 2\) (non-leading coefficients), and \(4 \nmid 2\) (constant term). Here the coefficients are \(1, 2, 0, 4, 2\): \(p=2\) divides \(2, 0, 4, 2\) but not the leading \(1\), and \(p^2=4\) does not divide the constant \(2\). So it is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

answer.text: Reducible: factors into two quadratics over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

name.text: PYQ Part B - Integral Domain has No Zero Divisors

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

A commutative ring \(R\) with unity is an integral domain if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

An integral domain has no zero divisors: \(ab = 0 \Rightarrow a=0\) or \(b=0\). Equivalently, the cancellation law holds: \(ab=ac\) and \(a\neq 0\) implies \(b=c\). Examples: \(\mathbb{Z}, \mathbb{Q}, \mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}\), and \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\). Note: \(\mathbb{Z}_6\) is NOT an integral domain since \(2\cdot3=0\) in \(\mathbb{Z}_6\).

answer.text: Every element has a multiplicative inverse
It has no zero divisors (\(ab = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0\) or \(b = 0\))

name.text: PYQ Part B - Number of Elements in Finite Field GF(p^n)

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

A finite field exists with exactly \(q\) elements if and only if \(q\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Finite fields (Galois fields) exist with exactly \(p^n\) elements for any prime \(p\) and positive integer \(n\), and for no other cardinalities. The unique (up to isomorphism) field with \(p^n\) elements is denoted \(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}\) or \(\text{GF}(p^n)\). Any two finite fields with the same number of elements are isomorphic.

answer.text: A prime power: \(q = p^n\) for some prime \(p\) and \(n \geq 1\)

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 2 - Algebra, Complex Analysis, Topology/Part B - Topology

name.text: PYQ Part B - Hausdorff Space Separation

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

A topological space \(X\) is Hausdorff (\(T_2\)) if and only if for any two distinct points \(x, y \in X\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Hausdorff (T2) spaces: for distinct \(x, y\), there exist disjoint open sets \(U \ni x\) and \(V \ni y\). Key facts: metric spaces are Hausdorff; compact subsets of Hausdorff spaces are closed; Hausdorff implies \(T_1\) (singletons are closed). Compact Hausdorff spaces have many pleasant properties.

answer.text: There is a continuous path from \(x\) to \(y\)
There exist disjoint open sets \(U \ni x\) and \(V \ni y\)
There is an open set containing both \(x\) and \(y\)

name.text: PYQ Part B - Compact Subset of Hausdorff Space is Closed

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

In a Hausdorff topological space, every compact subset is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Every compact subset of a Hausdorff space is closed. Proof: if \(K\) is compact and \(x \notin K\), for each \(k \in K\) separate \(x\) and \(k\) by disjoint open sets; use compactness to get a finite cover showing \(x\) has a neighborhood disjoint from \(K\). In non-Hausdorff spaces, compact sets need not be closed.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Tychonoff Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The Tychonoff theorem states that an arbitrary product of topological spaces is compact if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Tychonoff's theorem: the product \(\prod_{\alpha} X_\alpha\) (with product topology) is compact iff every \(X_\alpha\) is compact. It holds for arbitrary (even uncountable) index sets. The proof uses the axiom of choice (Zorn's lemma). It is one of the most important theorems in topology.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Connected Space Path-Connected

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Which of the following implications is TRUE for topological spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Path-connected \(\Rightarrow\) connected (always true: if \(X\) is path-connected and \(X = A \cup B\) is a separation, then a path between \(a \in A\) and \(b \in B\) gives a connected set meeting both open sets, contradiction). But connected does NOT imply path-connected (counterexample: the topologist's sine curve \(\{(x,\sin(1/x)): x>0\} \cup \{0\}\times[-1,1]\) is connected but not path-connected).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Quotient Topology

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The quotient space \([0,1]/\{0,1\}\) (obtained by identifying the two endpoints of \([0,1]\)) is homeomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Identifying the endpoints 0 and 1 of \([0,1]\) glues them together, creating a loop. The resulting quotient space is homeomorphic to the circle \(S^1\). Similarly, \([0,1]^2\) with opposite edges identified gives a torus.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 2 - Algebra, Complex Analysis, Topology/Part C - Complex Analysis

name.text: PYQ Part C - Rouché's Theorem Application

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

By Rouché's theorem, the number of zeros of \(f(z) = z^5 + 3z + 1\) inside the circle \(|z| = 2\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

Rouché's theorem: if \(|h(z)| < |g(z)|\) on \(C\), then \(g\) and \(g+h\) have the same number of zeros inside \(C\). On \(|z|=2\): \(|z^5| = 32\) and \(|3z+1| \leq 3(2)+1=7 < 32\). Take \(g=z^5\), \(h=3z+1\). So \(z^5+3z+1\) has the same number of zeros as \(z^5\) inside \(|z|=2\), which is \(5\).

name.text: PYQ Part C - Residue at Essential Singularity e^(1/z)

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The residue of \(f(z) = e^{1/z}\) at \(z = 0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Laurent series: \(e^{1/z} = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{1}{n!\,z^n} = 1 + \frac{1}{z} + \frac{1}{2z^2} + \cdots\). The residue is the coefficient of \(1/z\), which is \(1\). Note: \(z=0\) is an essential singularity (principal part has infinitely many terms).

name.text: PYQ Part C - Contour Integral of 1/(z^2+1)

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Using the residue theorem, \(\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{1+x^2}\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

The function \(\frac{1}{1+z^2}\) has poles at \(z = \pm i\). The upper half-plane contains the pole \(z=i\). Residue at \(z=i\): \(\lim_{z\to i}(z-i)\frac{1}{(z-i)(z+i)} = \frac{1}{2i}\). By residue theorem: integral \(= 2\pi i \cdot \frac{1}{2i} = \pi\). This matches the known result \(\arctan(x)\Big|_{-\infty}^{\infty} = \pi\).

name.text: PYQ Part C - Open Mapping Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The Open Mapping Theorem states that if \(f\) is a non-constant analytic function on a domain \(D \subset \mathbb{C}\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Open Mapping Theorem: every non-constant analytic function on a domain maps open sets to open sets. This is a deep result — it fails for real differentiable functions (e.g., \(f(x)=x^2\) maps \((-1,1)\) to \([0,1)\), which is not open). As a corollary, the image of a bounded domain cannot be contained in the boundary.

answer.text: \(f\) maps open subsets of \(D\) to open subsets of \(\mathbb{C}\)
\(f\) maps closed sets to closed sets
\(f\) maps connected sets to connected sets only

name.text: PYQ Part C - Conformal Map Upper Half-Plane to Disk

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

A conformal map from the upper half-plane \(\mathbb{H} = \{z : \text{Im}(z) > 0\}\) to the open unit disk \(\mathbb{D} = \{w : |w| < 1\}\) is given by:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

The Cayley map \(w = \frac{z-i}{z+i}\) maps \(\mathbb{H}\) conformally onto \(\mathbb{D}\). When \(\text{Im}(z) > 0\): \(|z-i|^2 = x^2+(y-1)^2 < x^2+(y+1)^2 = |z+i|^2\), so \(|w|<1\). The inverse is \(z = i\frac{1+w}{1-w}\).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 2 - Algebra, Complex Analysis, Topology/Part C - Group and Ring Theory

name.text: PYQ Part C - Sylow Third Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Let \(G\) be a group of order \(15\). By the Sylow theorems, \(G\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

By Sylow: the number \(n_5\) of Sylow 5-subgroups divides 3 and \(\equiv 1 \pmod 5\), so \(n_5 = 1\). Similarly \(n_3 \equiv 1 \pmod 3\) and \(n_3 \mid 5\), giving \(n_3 = 1\). Both Sylow subgroups are normal, so \(G \cong \mathbb{Z}_3 \times \mathbb{Z}_5 \cong \mathbb{Z}_{15}\), which is cyclic.

name.text: PYQ Part C - Simple Group A_5

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The alternating group \(A_5\) (even permutations of 5 elements) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

\(A_5\) has order \(5!/2 = 60\) and is simple (no proper non-trivial normal subgroups). It is the smallest non-abelian simple group. The simplicity of \(A_5\) shows that the general quintic polynomial is not solvable by radicals (Galois theory). \(A_n\) is simple for all \(n \geq 5\).

answer.text: Simple (has no proper non-trivial normal subgroup)

name.text: PYQ Part C - Galois Group of x^4 - 2

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The degree of the splitting field of \(x^4 - 2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

The roots of \(x^4-2\) are \(\sqrt[4]{2}, i\sqrt[4]{2}, -\sqrt[4]{2}, -i\sqrt[4]{2}\). The splitting field is \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[4]{2}, i)\). We have \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[4]{2}):\mathbb{Q}] = 4\) (since \(x^4-2\) is irreducible by Eisenstein) and \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[4]{2}, i):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[4]{2})] = 2\). So the degree is \(4 \times 2 = 8\), and the Galois group is the dihedral group \(D_4\) of order 8.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 2 - Algebra, Complex Analysis, Topology/Part C - Topology

name.text: PYQ Part C - Urysohn Metrization Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

By Urysohn's Metrization Theorem, a second-countable regular (\(T_3\)) Hausdorff space is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Urysohn's Metrization Theorem: every second-countable, regular (T3), Hausdorff space is metrizable (homeomorphic to a subset of the Hilbert cube \([0,1]^\omega\)). Equivalently, a second-countable normal Hausdorff space is metrizable. This gives a topological characterisation of metrizability in the separable case.

name.text: PYQ Part C - Fundamental Group of Circle

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The fundamental group \(\pi_1(S^1)\) of the circle \(S^1\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

\(\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}\). Loops on \(S^1\) are classified by their winding number (an integer). The generator is a loop that winds once around the circle. For spheres: \(\pi_1(S^n) = 0\) for \(n \geq 2\). For the torus: \(\pi_1(T^2) \cong \mathbb{Z}^2\).

name.text: PYQ Part C - Connectedness of GL_n(R)

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The general linear group \(GL_n(\mathbb{R}) = \{A \in M_n(\mathbb{R}) : \det A \neq 0\}\) with the subspace topology from \(M_n(\mathbb{R}) \cong \mathbb{R}^{n^2}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

\(GL_n(\mathbb{R})\) is NOT connected: it has two connected components separated by the sign of the determinant — matrices with \(\det > 0\) (including \(I\)) and matrices with \(\det < 0\). Each component is path-connected. The component of the identity is \(GL_n^+(\mathbb{R}) = \{A : \det A > 0\}\). In contrast, \(GL_n(\mathbb{C})\) is connected.

answer.text: Disconnected with exactly two path-connected components

name.text: PYQ Part C - Compact Metric Space is Complete and Totally Bounded

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

A metric space \((X,d)\) is compact if and only if it is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

For metric spaces: compact \(\Leftrightarrow\) complete and totally bounded \(\Leftrightarrow\) sequentially compact (every sequence has a convergent subsequence). Totally bounded means: for every \(\epsilon>0\), \(X\) can be covered by finitely many balls of radius \(\epsilon\). This characterisation fails for general topological spaces.
